steps.title: An LED that listens to you

narration.default: Every doorbell, every elevator button, every keyboard key on your laptop — they all do one tiny thing: turn an electrical signal on while you press, and off when you let go. Today you'll teach an LED that exact trick. Press the button, the light comes on. Release it, the light goes off. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you are in Upload mode. If you have not done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first. When you are ready, click Next.

Open the Arduino category in the toolbox and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts — like getting dressed in the morning. Loop runs over and over forever — like your heartbeat.
Drag a pinMode block into the setup section. Set pin to 13 and mode to OUTPUT. Output means the Arduino will SEND electricity out on this pin — that's how it lights up the LED.

steps.title: Tell pin 2 it's an input — with a twist

narration.default: Drag a second pinMode block below the first one. Set pin to 2 and mode to INPUT_PULLUP. INPUT_PULLUP is a clever trick — Arduino keeps the pin gently held HIGH all on its own, and pressing the button pulls it down to LOW. So a pressed button reads LOW. That feels backwards, but it's the most reliable way to wire a button.

Open the Control category and drag an if / else block into the loop section. This is the very first block in your tutorials that DECIDES — the if branch runs when the condition is true, otherwise the else branch runs. Two paths, one chosen each time the loop runs.

steps.title: Read what's on pin 2 right now

narration.default: Back in the Arduino category, drag a digitalRead block. Set its pin to 2. digitalRead is the opposite of digitalWrite — instead of sending a signal out, it asks 'what's on this pin right now?' and reports back HIGH or LOW.

steps.title: Build the question: is the button pressed?

narration.default: Open the Operators category and drag an equals block. Put digitalRead(2) on the left side and a LOW block (from the Arduino category) on the right side. Then drop the whole equals block into the condition slot of the if / else. Remember the pull-up trick — a pressed button reads LOW. So the question now reads: 'is pin 2 LOW right now?' which is the same as 'is the button pressed?'
Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch (the top one). Set pin to 13 and value to HIGH. This is what runs when the button is pressed — light on.

Drag another digitalWrite block into the else branch. Keep pin at 13 but set value to LOW. This is the otherwise — when the button is NOT pressed, the LED stays off. The code is finished — let's wire up the simulator.
Open the Simulator tab and click the plus button at the bottom right to open the component picker. We need to add the LED and the button to the virtual board.
Select LED from the component list. In the pin mapping dialog, choose pin 13 and click Save. Same pin your code is writing HIGH and LOW to.

steps.title: Add a push button on pin 2

narration.default: Open the component picker again and select Push Button. In the pin mapping dialog, choose pin 2 and click Save. The button is wired to ground — exactly what INPUT_PULLUP expects.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program will compile and load onto the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds.

Click and hold the simulated button. The LED turns on. Release it and the LED turns off. You just built your first program that REACTS to the world. Try this: swap HIGH and LOW in the two digitalWrite blocks — now your button turns the LED off and releasing turns it on. Or add a second LED on pin 12 and make it light up only when the button is released.
